images.caption:
Figure 1.C1 reward heatmap: best-pipeline score for each (reward function,
dataset) pair evaluated by an exhaustive greedy search over 112 ordered
pipeline sequences at MCAR 15%.
Trivial-collapse rewards (R1, R6a, R6b) saturate at≈1.0\approx 1.0uniformly
across all datasets (dark red), masking any discriminative signal.
Genuinely calibrated rewards (R2, R3, R7) exhibit dataset-specific
variation, making pipeline ranking meaningful.

Figure 2.C1 per-dataset scatter: best-pipeline reward score (X) vs. TabPFN v2
accuracy (Y) at MCAR 15%. Each point is one (dataset, reward function)
pair. Collapsed rewards (R1, R6a, R6b) cluster atx≈1.0x\approx 1.0with
variable TabPFN v2 accuracy, exposing the disconnect between reward
saturation and downstream quality.

Figure 3.C1 aggregated scatter: mean±\pmSD best-pipeline reward score vs. mean±\pmSD TabPFN v2 accuracy per reward function (D1–D10, MCAR 15%).TFMAwareReward(R7, black hexagon) achieves both the highest mean reward and the
highest mean TabPFN v2 accuracy, confirming reward–quality alignment.

Figure 4.C2 pipeline divergence: accuracy and ECE for the 4 datasets whereTFMAwareRewardand RF-reward choose different imputers (D2, D4, D5, D8).Δ\Delta= TFM−-RF; greenΔ>0\Delta>0favours TFM.
The 6 agreeing datasets are listed below.

Figure 5.C3: mean TabPFN v2 accuracy (left) and ECE (right) across D3–D5, D7, D8
under four error types (MCAR/MAR 15%, outlier/duplicate 10%).
Lower ECE is better.

Figure 6.C4 error sensitivity: TabPFN v2 accuracy vs. MCAR rate
(0–30%30\%in 6 steps) for D1, D3, D5, D7, D9.
B0 (grey), B1 (blue), B-greedy-TFM/ours (orange).

Figure 7.C5: best-found MultiObjectiveReward score per dataset (MCAR 15%).
Orange: parameterized actions (17); blue: discrete (7).
Annotations:Δ\Delta= parameterized−-discrete.

Figure 8.C6 transfer learning: episode reward vs. fine-tuning steps on D8–D10.
Orange: policy pre-trained on D3, then fine-tuned.
Blue dashed: trained from scratch. Red dotted: 2K-step checkpoint.
